div.head: ES–39. Telegram from the Chargé in El Salvador (Downs) to the Department of StateSource: Department of State, Central Files, 716.00/11–236. Confidential; Niact; Limit Distribution.
div.p: 326. For Mann. Following from Stewart:C. Allan Stewart, Counselor of Embassy in Venezuela, visited San Salvador, November 20–23. A memorandum from Stewart to Mann, December 2 reporting on this visit, was enclosed with a letter from Stewart to Higdon, December 2 1960. (ARA/OAP Files, Lot 64 D 16, “Junta Correspondence: El Salvador, 1960.”)

After two hour meeting with Osorio, two hour session attended by all six members of Junta and about 15 interviews Salvadorans, Americans and others these are my observations: 1)
Osorio was key figure in overthrow. He and civilian elements were in accord sometime before overthrow. 2)
Osorio admits there will probably be counter attempts in armed forces and plotting is in evidence but based on my information Osorio has enough support in armed forces to keep present Government in power. Osorio has receded into background and provides occasional advice but three military members of Junta are his men and Minister InteriorCol. Alberto Escamilla. is strong type loyal to Osorio. Therefore, Osorio is key man in country. 3)
Civilian members of Junta are well-intentioned but inexperienced. I feel they are anxious to cooperate with U.S. If there were any doubt, however, Junta setup provides excellent check and balance with military members, backed by Osorio, exerting deciding influence. My meeting with Junta members was marked by frank exchange of ideas, especially on appointment people of doubtful ideology to Government
posts. They are aware of background these people and reached unanimous decision appoint them after
long debate. After conversing many serious Salvadorans I conclude there are sharply diverging opinions about whether doubtful individuals are Communists. In any event, Junta is aware our concern for some appointees and Osorio welcomed memorandum [text not declassified]The paper entitled, “Cuban Subersive Activities in E1 Salvador,” was not found in Department of State files. When translated “Osorio suggested it be given to Minister Interior Escamilla. 4)
Osorio and Junta are for free elections. They think Democratic forces can overwhelm Leftists at polls, and Osorio starts organizing new party to attract bulk of PRUD on Friday, with big convention around December 15. Civilian Junta members told me they discussed two types overthrow in first stages of plotting, namely à la Castro or à la Betancourt. Decided on latter because by Democratic forces in Venezuela won out later in free elections. Said this formula also had been successful in Guatemala and Honduras. Osorio said PRAM and CGTS will be controlled. Close friend one of military members Junta and of Minister Interior said these two strongly confirmed this fact to him. However, same source, evidencing his own concern, added that he had asked his friends if they sure they not being used. 5)
Junta admits its functions as caretaker Government while Congressional elections prepared is not enough and must make start on social program. Osorio said he had ideas along this line. 6)
Reaction among Salvadorans with whom I talked is regret for departure constitutional process but strong to mild feeling Lemus bungled student disturbances so badly his removal was logical aftermath. Return to constitutional Government is avowed unanimous desire. 7)
Consensus opinion is that any succeeding Government would be rightist military Junta. Some observers viewed this with alarm, saying it would not be accepted by liberal elements and masses, with consequent trouble.
8)
I am most impressed with attitude of intelligent Salvadorans who realize dangers of present situation and wish cooperate with Junta in finding solution along peaceful lines. Meanwhile working class continues at tasks without interruption. Cumbersome six-man Junta may be streamlined but I believe only after satisfactory agreement between civilians and military. 9)
Osorio made plea for recognition and Junta members were most eager get over point they wish to work with U.S. and need our support.
